chapter_title: Pulp of wood or of other fibrous cellulosic material; recovered (waste and scrap) paper or paperboard
scope_note: Note 1: 'chemical wood pulp, dissolving grades' (47.02) = chemical wood pulp with INSOLUBLE FRACTION ≥92% (soda/sulphate pulp) or ≥88% (sulphite pulp) after 1 hour in 18% NaOH at 20°C; AND for sulphite pulp: ash content ≤0.15% by weight. Chapter 47 covers upstream raw materials (pulp) for paper/paperboard production (Ch.48) or for man-made fibre production (Ch.54/55).
inclusions: Heading 47.01 — Mechanical wood pulp: groundwood, thermomechanical (TMP), chemi-thermomechanical (CTMP). Produced by grinding/refining wood without chemical treatment. High yield but lower quality (used for newsprint, tissue)

Heading 47.02 — Chemical wood pulp, dissolving grades. Note 1: soda/sulphate pulp with ≥92% insoluble fraction in 18% NaOH; OR sulphite pulp ≥88% insoluble fraction + ash ≤0.15%. Used for viscose/rayon and cellulose acetate production (feeds Ch.54/55 man-made fibres)

Heading 47.03 — Chemical wood pulp, soda or sulphate, other than dissolving grades: unbleached and bleached/semi-bleached varieties. High purity, used for quality paper/paperboard (kraft paper, bleached board for packaging)
Heading 47.04 — Chemical wood pulp, sulphite, other than dissolving grades: unbleached and bleached/semi-bleached. Lower lignin content than soda/sulphate, used for fine papers
Heading 47.05 — Semi-chemical wood pulp: produced by combining chemi-mechanical methods. NSSC (Neutral Sulphite Semi-Chemical) is the main type, used primarily for corrugating medium

Heading 47.06 — Pulps of fibres derived by recovering and recycling waste paper or paperboard, including pulps of other fibrous cellulosic materials (cotton linters pulp, flax pulp, esparto/alfa pulp, straw pulp, bamboo pulp, sugarcane bagasse pulp)

Heading 47.07 — Recovered (waste and scrap) paper or paperboard: sorted paper waste (old newspapers ONP, old corrugated containers OCC, office waste, mixed paper) collected for recycling into new pulp/paper

exclusions.condition: Wood in log/chip/sawdust form (pre-pulping raw wood materials)
exclusions.action: Redirect to Chapter 44 (wood and articles of wood). Wood chips for pulping → 44.01. Only after pulping process does wood become Ch.47 pulp

exclusions.condition: Paper and paperboard (finished product from pulp)
exclusions.action: Redirect to Chapter 48 (paper and paperboard). Pulp → Ch.47. Paper made from pulp → Ch.48. The conversion process (wet forming on paper machine) moves material from Ch.47 to Ch.48

exclusions.condition: Chemical wood pulp, dissolving grades (47.02) used as man-made fibre raw material — note on downstream use
exclusions.action: 47.02 itself stays in Ch.47. The DOWNSTREAM product (viscose/rayon/cellulose acetate) → Ch.54/55. The dissolving pulp is used as the cellulosic raw material for artificial man-made fibres

classification_rules.rule: Note 1 — 47.02 'Dissolving grades': strict alkali-solubility and ash test
classification_rules.description: Chemical wood pulp is classified as DISSOLVING GRADE (47.02) ONLY if it passes: SODA or SULPHATE PULP: ≥92% of fibres insoluble in 18% NaOH solution at 20°C for 1 hour. SULPHITE PULP: ≥88% insoluble fraction in same test AND ash content ≤0.15% by weight. Non-dissolving grades of soda/sulphate → 47.03. Non-dissolving sulphite → 47.04. The dissolving test measures cellulose purity — high purity needed for dissolving into viscose/acetate.

classification_rules.rule: Ch.47 heading matrix: pulping process type determines heading
classification_rules.description: Ch.47 headings are determined by the PULPING PROCESS and RECOVERY STATUS: 47.01 = MECHANICAL only (groundwood, TMP, CTMP — no chemicals for fibre liberation). 47.02 = CHEMICAL (soda/sulphate or sulphite) meeting DISSOLVING GRADE purity thresholds. 47.03 = CHEMICAL SODA/SULPHATE, non-dissolving (including kraft pulp — same chemicals as 47.02 but lower purity). 47.04 = CHEMICAL SULPHITE, non-dissolving. 47.05 = SEMI-CHEMICAL (combined mechanical + chemical, e.g. NSSC). 47.06 = NON-WOOD fibrous cellulosic pulp + recovered paper pulp (cotton linters, straw, bamboo, recycled fibre). 47.07 = RECOVERED WASTE PAPER (unsorted or sorted, pre-pulping).

classification_rules.rule: Key boundary: 47.07 (recovered waste paper) vs 48.xx (processed/recycled paper): pre-pulping vs post-pulping
classification_rules.description: The distinction between 47.07 and Ch.48: 47.07 = WASTE/SCRAP paper and paperboard collected for recycling — still in original paper form (old newspapers, old corrugated containers, mixed office waste) but designated for reprocessing. Ch.48 = PAPER AND PAPERBOARD that has already been manufactured (including recycled-content paper). Sorted waste paper waiting to go to the mill → 47.07. New paper made partly from recovered fibres → Ch.48. The physical form (original waste paper vs. newly manufactured sheet) determines the boundary.

classification_rules.rule: Key boundary: 47.02 (dissolving pulp) vs 47.03/47.04 (paper-grade chemical pulp): downstream use indicator
classification_rules.description: The dissolving grade / paper grade distinction is commercially significant: DISSOLVING GRADE (47.02) → used to make man-made fibres (viscose rayon, lyocell, cellulose acetate, carboxymethylcellulose) — requires very high cellulose purity (≥88–92% alkali-resistant). PAPER GRADE (47.03/47.04) → used to make paper/paperboard — lower purity specifications acceptable. Both use the same chemical processes (soda/sulphate = kraft; sulphite) but at different post-cooking bleaching/purification levels. The alkali-solubility test (Note 1) is the definitive chemical test.

classification_rules.rule: Key boundary: Ch.47 (pulp/waste paper) vs Ch.44 (wood raw material) vs Ch.48 (finished paper)
classification_rules.description: Three-stage production chain: RAW WOOD: chips, logs, sawdust → Ch.44 (44.01/44.03/44.07). After mechanical grinding or chemical cooking → PULP → Ch.47. After wet-forming on paper machine + drying → PAPER/PAPERBOARD → Ch.48. The pivotal conversion: wood → pulp at the pulp mill (Ch.44→Ch.47). Pulp → paper at the paper machine (Ch.47→Ch.48). Recovered paper sent for repulping → Ch.47 (47.07). Recovered paper made into new paper → Ch.48.
